metadata.summary: Use feature flags, branch by abstraction, and dark launching to deploy incomplete features on trunk daily without exposing them to users.

content.title: Trunk-Based Development: Patterns
content.summary: Implement multi-step features safely on trunk by using feature flags to hide incomplete work, branch by abstraction to refactor without long-lived branches, and dark launching to test new implementations against production traffic before exposure.

why.p: Feature flags decouple deployment from release: code reaches production quickly without breaking trunk, allowing agents to land small safe diffs frequently while keeping the CI gate tight.

Branch by Abstraction enables large refactoring without feature branches: create an abstraction layer, implement the new code behind the abstraction, and swap implementations via a flag. Once validated, delete the old code and the abstraction collapses back to direct calls.

Dark launching runs the new implementation in parallel without exposing results, comparing outcomes before traffic routing switches. This pattern catches subtle bugs (float precision, list ordering, non-deterministic behavior) that pass unit tests but fail on real data.
These patterns allow teams to maintain a fast, releasable trunk while implementing complex features incrementally. They are the foundation of trunk-based development at scale.

list.item: Agent is implementing a multi-step feature behind a flag (Keystone Interface, Branch by Abstraction) — give it this reference for the pattern shape.
Setting up the pre-commit hook + CI pipeline so trunk stays releasable; the YAML/.pre-commit-config.yaml blocks are copy-pastable.
Dark-launch a new implementation in parallel and diff results — the snippet is the canonical shape.
Migrating a long-lived feature branch to a series of trunk-merged increments; agent uses Branch-by-Abstraction to chunk it.
Branching/lifetime principles, DORA metrics, GitFlow comparison — see sibling trunk-based-dev-principles.
Feature flag infrastructure itself (provider choice, evaluation engine) — see feature-flags methodology.
Release engineering for mobile/desktop where store review breaks daily-merge cadence.
Codebases with no test gate — TBD without tests is "trunk is always broken".

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Best practices for safe trunk-based patterns with feature flags and branch by abstraction:

rule.statement: Branch lifetime cap is under 24 hours. Rebase onto main before opening the PR; squash on merge so trunk history stays linear.
rule.rationale: Long-lived branches accumulate conflicts and diverge from the trunk, defeating the purpose of TBD. Squash merge keeps trunk history linear and searchable.

rule.statement: One flag = one task = one cleanup ticket. File the cleanup ticket the moment the flag is born; otherwise flags accumulate to dozens and rot.
rule.rationale: Flags with no removal deadline become permanent technical debt. Cleaning up immediately after rollout prevents flag sprawl.

rule.statement: Flag naming follows <area>.<verb>_<thing> (e.g., billing.use_stripe_v2); ban boolean negatives (disable_x) because they invert mid-rollout and confuse agents.
rule.rationale: Positive naming is unambiguous. A flag named "billing.disable_legacy" inverts logic when renamed to "billing.use_new" during rollout, causing off-by-one errors in agent-generated code.

rule.statement: CI must be under 10 minutes wall time. If slower, parallelize tests, cache dependencies, and move slow integration suites to nightly jobs. Slow CI kills TBD adoption.
rule.rationale: Developers and agents disable hooks or skip CI on slow feedback loops. Sub-10-minute CI sustains the daily-merge cadence.

rule.statement: Auto-revert on red trunk: a PR that broke main is reverted by bot (git revert -m 1), not by humans. Fix forward in a fresh PR.
rule.rationale: Manual reverts are slow and error-prone. Bot-driven reverts keep trunk green and force authors to fix in a follow-up, maintaining the discipline.

rule.statement: Forbid direct push to main from anyone except automation; require linear history and signed commits.
rule.rationale: Direct pushes bypass CI and introduce merge commits. Requiring PRs ensures every change passes gates and is reviewable.

rule.statement: Run dark launches at less than 1% traffic for at least one diurnal cycle before scaling. Bug surfaces look identical at 0.01% and 1% but error budgets diverge.
rule.rationale: Low-traffic canary windows catch bugs safely. Scaling to 1% too quickly masks bugs that only appear at scale.

section.name: feature-flags
section.p: Feature flags hide incomplete features behind conditional logic so they can deploy to production without exposing them. Flags support global on/off, percentage rollout, and user-specific targeting.

example.title: Feature flag hiding a new feature
example.code:
# Hide incomplete features behind flags # Deploy to production without exposing
from feature_flags import is_enabled
def get_user_profile(user_id: str): profile = fetch_profile(user_id)
# New feature behind flag if is_enabled("show_activity_feed", user_id): profile["activity"] = fetch_activity(user_id)
return profile
# Feature flag service class FeatureFlags: def __init__(self, provider: FlagProvider): self.provider = provider
def is_enabled(self, flag: str, user_id: str = None) -> bool: """ Check if feature is enabled. Supports: - Global on/off - Percentage rollout - User-specific targeting """ return self.provider.evaluate(flag, {"user_id": user_id})

antipattern.description: Pattern code is illustrative — is_enabled("name") assumes a real provider; agent will leave a stub and forget to wire it.
antipattern.reason: Hard-coded True makes the flag always-on in CI and production. Use env vars (FLAGS_DEFAULT=off in CI) to enforce safety and grep for literal is_enabled(...True).

section.name: branch-by-abstraction
section.p: Branch by Abstraction enables large refactoring without feature branches. Create an abstraction, implement both old and new behind it, and swap implementations via a flag. Once validated, delete the old code and collapse the abstraction.

example.title: Branch by Abstraction for payment refactor

antipattern.description: Branch-by-Abstraction snippet skips the cleanup phase ("delete LegacyPaymentProcessor"); agents drop the abstraction and never collapse it back, leaving permanent indirection.
antipattern.reason: Permanent indirection wastes compute and confuses future agents. Always file a cleanup task before merging the new implementation.

section.name: pre-commit-hooks
section.p: Pre-commit hooks catch issues before pushing to trunk, ensuring only quality code reaches the main branch:

example.title: Pre-commit configuration example
example.code:
# .pre-commit-config.yaml # Ensure quality before pushing to trunk
repos: - repo: local hooks: - id: tests name: Run tests entry: pytest --tb=short -q language: system pass_filenames: false
- id: lint name: Lint code entry: ruff check . language: system pass_filenames: false
- id: type-check name: Type check entry: mypy src/ language: system pass_filenames: false

section.note: Pre-commit example runs the full pytest on commit; on real repos this is too slow and devs disable the hook. Use pre-commit run --hook-stage properly or scope to changed files.

section.name: ci-pipeline
section.p: Fast CI pipeline provides rapid feedback, essential for trunk-based development. CI should complete in under 10 minutes to sustain daily-merge cadence.

example.title: CI pipeline for trunk-based development
example.code:
# .github/workflows/trunk.yml name: Trunk CI
on: push: branches: [main] pull_request: branches: [main]
jobs: build-and-test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4
- name: Setup Python uses: actions/setup-python@v5 with: python-version: '3.12'
- name: Install dependencies run: pip install -r requirements.txt
- name: Run tests run: pytest --cov=src --cov-fail-under=80
- name: Lint run: ruff check .
- name: Type check run: mypy src/
# Fast feedback is crucial # Total time should be < 10 minutes
deploy-to-staging: needs: build-and-test if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - name: Deploy to staging run: ./deploy.sh staging
# Optional: auto-deploy to production # deploy-to-production: # needs: deploy-to-staging # ...

section.note: CI snippet has no branch protection / required-status check details — agent forgets to set "Require status checks" on main.

section.name: keystone-interface
section.p: Build a complete feature in parts, adding each piece on trunk behind a flag. Only enable the flag when all parts are ready. This pattern is also called "incomplete interface" or "dark protocol".

example.title: Keystone Interface Pattern for activity API

section.name: dark-launching
section.p: Dark launching deploys the new implementation to production but does not expose it. Run it in parallel, compare results with the current implementation, and route traffic only when confident the new code is safe.

example.title: Dark Launch with result comparison
example.code:
# Deploy to production, but don't expose # Test with production traffic
async def process_order(order: Order): # Current implementation result = await current_processor.process(order)
# Dark launch: run new implementation in parallel if feature_flags.is_enabled("dark_launch_new_processor"): try: new_result = await new_processor.process(order) # Compare results, log differences if result != new_result: logger.warning(f"Result mismatch: {result} vs {new_result}") except Exception as e: logger.error(f"Dark launch error: {e}")
# Always return current result return result

antipattern.description: Dark-launch comparison code re-raises exceptions into production paths.
antipattern.reason: The dark path should never crash the current path. Wrap in try/except at all costs; the README example does, agents often skip it.

section.note: Dark-launch comparison only works for deterministic, equality-comparable outputs — float / list-order cases need a tolerant diff.

section.name: common-challenges
section.p: Common challenges implementing trunk-based development and how to address them:

rule.statement: Trunk is Always Broken: Fix by gating merges on passing tests, adding pre-commit checks, speeding up CI to under 10 minutes, using feature flags for incomplete work, and keeping commits small.
rule.rationale: Fast feedback + safety gates + smaller changes = trust in trunk. Without these, developers work around TBD.

rule.statement: Need Long-Lived Branches: Fix by breaking features into smaller parts, implementing feature flags, using Branch by Abstraction, and reserving long-lived branches only for release branches or compliance gating.
rule.rationale: Long-lived branches are an anti-pattern in TBD. Chunking and flags eliminate the need for most branch lifetimes over 24 hours.

rule.statement: Code Review Bottleneck: Fix by keeping PRs under 200 lines, pairing instead of async review, enforcing a fast SLA (under 4 hours), and rotating reviewers to spread load.
rule.rationale: Small PRs are faster to review and merge. Async review is slow; live review (pair or sync check-in) unblocks faster.

section.name: integration-with-sdd
section.p: Trunk-based patterns integrate naturally with SDD (Semantic-Driven Development) task execution:

rule.statement: TBD + SDD Workflow: create task in .aidocs/backlog, move to .aidocs/todo, create short-lived branch (less than 1 day), implement with feature flags, open PR, fast review, merge to trunk, move task to .aidocs/done.
rule.rationale: Each SDD task = one trunk-merged increment. Feature flags control visibility; cleanup tasks surface flag removal.

rule.statement: Feature Flags for SDD: flag name matches task ID when possible, enable after thorough testing, remove after stability confirmed.
rule.rationale: Traceability: grep the codebase and find which task introduced each flag.

rule.statement: Continuous Delivery from Trunk: trunk push triggers CI, CI triggers staging deploy. Manual or approval-gated production deploy. Feature flags control visibility, not deployment. Task done = merged to trunk. Task deployed = feature flag enabled. Task complete = flag removed.
rule.rationale: Deployment and release are decoupled. Code deploys to production frequently; flags determine what end-users see.

section.name: tools-and-services
section.p: CLI tools for trunk-based development and flag management:

rule.statement: gh (GitHub CLI) — manage PRs and branch protection from CLI. https://cli.github.com
pre-commit — local commit-time gates. pip install pre-commit · https://pre-commit.com
lefthook — faster Go-based git hooks. https://github.com/evilmartians/lefthook
git-absorb — auto-fixup commits onto right base, keeps PRs clean. https://github.com/tummychow/git-absorb
gh-stack / git-spice / Graphite CLI — stacked PRs (small increments). https://graphite.dev/cli
act — run GitHub Actions locally before push. https://github.com/nektos/act
flagsmith-cli / unleash-edge / growthbook — manage flags from CLI. Per provider.
LaunchDarkly — SaaS, industry default for flag infra; agents can flip flags via ldcli. REST + webhooks.
Flagsmith — SaaS / OSS, REST, OSS self-host option; cheaper.
Unleash — OSS, REST, self-hosted, Apache-2.0.
GrowthBook — OSS / SaaS, REST, pairs flagging with experimentation.
Statsig — SaaS, REST, flags + experimentation; generous free tier.
GitHub branch protection — SaaS, REST/GraphQL, required statuses + required reviews enforce TBD.
ConfigCat — SaaS, REST, lightweight, simple.
Split.io — SaaS, REST, enterprise flagging + ramps.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: The agent's job is to land small, safe diffs frequently. Use this reference when generating the wrapper code for a flagged change: read the parent feature's spec, write the smallest increment, gate it with a flag, ship via short-lived branch, and surface the cleanup task once the flag is rolled to 100%. Maintain a tracking artifact (.aidocs/feature-flags.md or similar) so flags don't outlive their reason.

rule.statement: Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent (natural fit: it already enforces task lifecycle and quality gates aligned to "task done = merged to trunk"), faion-feature-executor (for the sequential task chain inside one feature, with each task = one trunk merge).

Prompt pattern: "Implement payment-rewrite increment 3/5 using Branch by Abstraction. Reference: solo/dev/automation-tooling/trunk-based-dev-patterns/README.md. Constraints: abstraction PaymentProcessor exists in src/billing/abc.py; add NewStripeProcessor; gate via flag 'billing.use_new_processor'; DO NOT delete LegacyPaymentProcessor yet; update CHANGELOG, open SDD subtask 'remove flag billing.use_new_processor'; branch: feat/billing-stripe-3; squash-merge same day."

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common mistakes agents make when implementing trunk-based patterns:

rule.statement: LLMs forget to remove the abstraction layer after rollout; always file the "collapse abstraction" follow-up before merging the introduction.
rule.rationale: Without explicit cleanup, the temporary abstraction becomes permanent indirection.

rule.statement: Agent generates flag checks with hard-coded True defaults during local dev and forgets to revert — gate via env (FLAGS_DEFAULT=off in CI) and grep for literal is_enabled(... True).
rule.rationale: Hard-coded True makes the flag always-on in CI and production, defeating the purpose.

rule.statement: Dark-launch comparison code re-raises in production paths — wrap in try/except at all costs; the README example does, the agent often skips it.
rule.rationale: The dark path should never crash the current path.

rule.statement: Squash-merge loses commit-level granularity; agents writing 200-line PRs as one commit are fine, agents writing 2000-line PRs need a stacked-PR workflow instead.
rule.rationale: Squash commit enables size enforcement.

rule.statement: Agent will "fix" pre-commit failures by adding # type: ignore / # noqa instead of fixing the issue; require diff review before any new pragma.
rule.rationale: Pragmas suppress the tool; they do not fix the underlying issue.

rule.statement: Human-in-loop checkpoint: any flag flip from less than 50% to 100% on a billing/auth-critical path. Agent can stage the rollout but a human approves the final percent.
rule.rationale: Critical systems warrant extra caution.

rule.statement: Branch protection state drift: agent adding a job to CI may leave main not requiring it — verify via gh api repos/:owner/:repo/branches/main/protection after CI changes.
rule.rationale: Without branch protection verification, new CI checks become advisory, not mandatory.

section.name: examples-and-patterns
section.p: Real-world examples and common patterns for implementing trunk-based development:

example.title: Basic implementation with configuration
example.code:
# Basic example implementation
# Setup config = { "option1": "value1", "option2": "value2" }
# Execute result = implement_feature(config)
# Verify print(f"Result: {result}")

example.title: Production-ready implementation with error handling
Integration with external services
example.code:
# Integration example
class IntegratedImplementation: """Integration with existing systems."""
def __init__(self, config: Dict, external_service): self.config = config self.external_service = external_service
def execute(self) -> Dict: """Execute with external service integration."""
# Fetch data from external service external_data = self.external_service.fetch_data()
# Process with this methodology processed = self.process(external_data)
# Send results back self.external_service.send_results(processed)
return {"status": "success", "items_processed": len(processed)}
def process(self, data: list) -> list: """Process data using methodology.""" results = [] for item in data: result = apply_methodology(item) results.append(result) return results

example.title: Performance-optimized async implementation
example.code:
# Performance-optimized example
import asyncio from concurrent.futures import ThreadPoolExecutor from typing import List
async def optimized_implementation(items: List[str]) -> List[Dict]: """Async implementation for better performance."""
# Process items in parallel with ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=10) as executor: futures = [ executor.submit(process_item, item) for item in items ]
# Gather results results = [future.result() for future in futures]
return results
def process_item(item: str) -> Dict: """Process a single item.""" return {"item": item, "status": "processed"}

example.title: Error recovery decorator
Caching pattern
Monitoring pattern
example.code:
# Common Pattern: Monitoring
from time import time
def with_monitoring(func): """Add monitoring to function.""" def wrapper(*args, **kwargs): start = time() try: result = func(*args, **kwargs) duration = time() - start logger.info(f"{func.__name__} completed in {duration:.2f}s") return result except Exception as e: duration = time() - start logger.error(f"{func.__name__} failed after {duration:.2f}s: {e}") raise return wrapper

section.name: templates-and-configuration
section.p: Templates and configuration examples for setting up trunk-based development patterns:

example.title: Basic configuration template
example.code:
# Basic configuration template # Replace placeholders with your values
name: "your-project-name" version: "1.0.0"
# Configuration options options: option1: value1 option2: value2

example.title: Advanced configuration with environments
example.code:
# Advanced configuration with all options
name: "your-project-name" version: "1.0.0"
# Environment-specific settings environments: development: setting1: dev-value production: setting1: prod-value
# Advanced options advanced: caching: true monitoring: true optimization: true

example.title: Feature implementation template
Unit test template
example.code:
# Unit test template
import pytest
class TestFeature: """Test suite for feature."""
def test_basic_functionality(self): """Test basic use case.""" result = your_function() assert result == expected_value
def test_edge_case(self): """Test edge case.""" result = your_function(edge_case_input) assert result is not None
def test_error_handling(self): """Test error handling.""" with pytest.raises(ValueError): your_function(invalid_input)

content.reference: Templates and sample configurations for feature flags, branch-by-abstraction, and CI pipelines.

checklist.check: Review current practices and identify gaps.
Assess team readiness and define success metrics.
Train team members on TBD practices and tooling.
Set up automation and communicate changes.
Start with a pilot project and apply the methodology.
Optimize workflows and update best practices.
Review process regularly and train new team members.
For the first few weeks, monitor flag proliferation — enforce a rule that every flag has a removal task.
Train team (and agents) on the gotchas: cleanup after rollout, no hard-coded True, dark-launch safety, branch protection drift.
Implement feature flag tracking to ensure removal of completed flags.
Document branch protection rules and require code review before merge.

see-also.link: Accelerate: The Science of Lean Software and DevOps
